result:
I want to write about the moment the sky forgets its own color. Not the cliché of a sunset bleeding out into bruised violet, but the subtler collapse that happens on a Tuesday in mid-October when the clouds have been arguing all morning and suddenly capitulate, turning the entire zenith into a sheet of photocopier paper left on the glass too long—gray that isn’t even interesting enough to be called pewter. I want to write about how that dullness seeps downward, puddles in the corners of storefront windows, smears across the windshield of a Buick idling outside a laundromat on the edge of a town whose name is always abbreviated by locals to a single confident syllable—“Spoff” or “Ming” or “Troy.” I want to claim that, in that moment, the Buick’s windshield becomes a cinema screen for every regret the driver never confessed, but I also want to admit that the driver is merely hungry, mildly inconvenienced, and checking his phone for a text that will not arrive for another seventeen months, at which point it will say only “Hey, found your sock,” and he will not remember which sock, or whose.

I want to write about the sock. Not the one that slips behind the drum of the dryer, but its twin that stays in the drawer, loyal, monogamous, baffled by abandonment, developing the lonely fuzz of fabric left too long unmated. I want to give that sock consciousness enough only to sense incompleteness, not to understand it, so that each sunrise feels like standing at the edge of a subway platform missing one shoe, hoping the other will clatter down the stairs before the express arrives. I want the sock to smell inexplicably of cedar and chlorine, to carry a faint rust-colored ghost of someone’s ankle on its inside curve. I want it to be folded—no, rolled—into a ball with its own cuff, the way my grandmother taught me, so it looks like a small mute swan waiting for a sound it will never make.

I want to write about the sound a city makes when it exhales at 3:07 a.m., the precise instant when the last bar dumps its drunks into the street, when the traffic lights click into their flashing cautionary yellow, when a bicyclist coasts through—no hands—singing a song from 1987 that he does not realize he knows. I want to argue that the collective sigh is measurable on the Richter scale of sociology, that somewhere in the basement of a university library a graduate student wearing mismatched socks (see above) is logging that exhalation into a spreadsheet that will one day prove the city is more organism than infrastructure, will earn him a dissertation no one reads except his mother, who will underline the phrase “biotic respiration of asphalt” and feel proud without understanding why.

I want to write about the underside of bridges, the way limestone sweats stalactites of rust, the way pigeons arrange themselves like black and white notes on an unreadable score. I want to claim that every bridge keeps a secret diary in the language of vibration, that when the semi-trucks rumble overhead the sentences get longer, full of dependent clauses about weight limits and December salt, and that on Sundays the paragraphs shorten into haiku about the river below: “Steel over water / a heron keeps its own counsel / the moon practices drowning.” I want to believe that the city’s engineers have a poet on retainer who translates these journals into mandates: add a girder here, subtract a joint there, caulk the whisper that says everything eventually falls.

I want to write about eventually. About how long it takes for a plastic grocery bag to admit it is no longer a bag but a flag of civic shame caught on the uppermost limb of a maple that predates the subdivision. About the moment it realizes it will never again hold bread, will never again ride the dark of a trunk, will only slowly bleach into tatters that look, from a distance, like the wings of an egret that has misread its migratory calendar. I want to give the bag one single memory: the press of a warm container of milk against its polyethylene flank, the way the cap had a thread of foil that caught the light like a miniature lightning bolt. I want the bag to guard that memory the way a monk guards a relic, repeating it in the wind until the words wear holes in themselves.

I want to write about words wearing holes. About how “awesome” once meant inducing awe, how now it means “I acknowledge your text,” how soon it will be the name of a laundry detergent that promises to remove existential stains. I want to track the migration of “lol” from laughter to lubricant of silence, the way it slips into the space where a chuckle would be if we still made sound. I want to argue that letters are atoms with half-lives, that the e in “email” has already decayed into a faint click of the tongue, that in fifty years children will find a fossilized @ in the chalk cliffs and wonder which mollusk spiraled like that.

I want to write about cliffs that are not cliffs but the edges of quarries flooded into lakes the color of windshield water. I want to stand at the lip with a teenager who holds a cigarette he never intends to light, who wears a T-shirt commemorating a concert that happened before his parents met. I want him to tell me the quarry is bottomless, not as metaphor but as measurable fact: divers went down with reels of twine and ran out before they touched silt. I want to believe that somewhere down there is a Woolworth’s sign, a Studebaker, the front half of a carnival horse from a merry-go-round, all preserved in the cold absence of light, waiting for the season when the water forgets its index of refraction and the whole town sees, for one instant, the past shimmering like a mirage it mistakes for tomorrow.

I want to write about tomorrow, but only the version that exists in the five seconds before the alarm rings, when the dream collapses into the pillow and the pillow refuses to testify. I want to subpoena the pillow, to cross-examine it about why some mornings we wake with the taste of a place we have never been—say, a citrus grove near Valencia in 1962—still on our tongues. I want the pillow to plead the fifth, to remain uncooperative and fluffy, and I want the prosecutor—who looks suspiciously like my seventh-grade English teacher—to slam her hand on the lectern and declare, “The state rests, but the state also wonders why declarative sentences keep curling into questions.”

I want to write about questions that curl like the rind of an orange peeled in one long spiral, how if you hold it up to the fluorescent light of a dorm kitchen it becomes a Möbius strip of scent, how the oils release a memory of a cafeteria in February where a boy once balanced sections of fruit on the rim of a Styrofoam cup like miniature suns. I want that boy to be decades older now, to have a daughter who calls fruit “snack oranges” because she thinks the word orange is only a color, and I want him to feel no sorrow about this, only an amused recognition that language is a deck of cards continually being shuffled by toddlers.

I want to write about toddlers who shuffle cards, about the moment they discover that the joker looks like their uncle when he laughs, about how they will spend the afternoon trying to push the card back into the box through the cellophane, insisting on cooperation from cardboard. I want to stand behind one such child, feel the heat of her concentration radiate like a tiny star, and I want the star to speak in a voice of gravel and helium: “The trick is not to make things fit, but to make fitting irrelevant.” I want to write that down, to tattoo it on the inside of my eyelid, to remember it the next time I try to cram my own life back into its original box.

I want to write about boxes that once held boots, the way they linger in closets like coffins for footwear, how they smell of paper and the distant stamp of a factory in Dongguan. I want to open one such box decades later and find not boots but a collection of paper cranes folded from parking tickets, each wing inked with the date and the temperature and the phrase “I’m sorry” in handwriting I almost recognize. I want to unfold one crane, to smooth the creases until the ticket becomes legible again, to discover that the fine was paid in full on the same day the sky forgot its color, the same day the Buick idled outside the laundromat, the same day the sock learned loneliness.

I want to write about that convergence, about how every small sadness is a spoke attached to the same hub, a hub that spins not like a wheel but like aLazy Susan in the center of a table where nobody serves themselves anymore. I want to clear the table, to set it with mismatched plates and the fork that has one bent tine, to invite the driver, the graduate student, the heron, the bag, the boy, the daughter, the joker, and the sock. I want the Lazy Susan to turn, to offer each of them the same dish—something with potatoes, something that steams—and I want the steam to rise until it clouds the window above the sink, until the window becomes a mirror, until the mirror fogs so completely that we finally see nothing but our own breath writing and rewriting the same word in the glass: stay.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
